question: Explain two ways in which discarded electronic waste (e-waste) can negatively impact oceanic food webs.
expectedPoints: Leaching of heavy metals (e.g., lead, mercury) from e-waste can contaminate water and sediments, entering the food chain through plankton and benthic organisms.
Ingestion of microplastics from degraded plastic e-waste components by filter feeders and small fish, leading to bioaccumulation of toxins in larger predators.

question: Analyze how the management of e-waste is an issue of environmental justice that directly connects to the health of oceanic ecosystems.
expectedPoints: A significant portion of the world's e-waste is exported from developed to developing nations, often to coastal regions with inadequate recycling infrastructure.
Primitive processing methods (e.g., open-air burning, acid baths) in these communities release toxic heavy metals and persistent organic pollutants (POPs) directly into local waterways.

These pollutants flow into coastal and marine environments, damaging local fisheries and coral reefs, while also disproportionately affecting the health of the marginalized communities performing the recycling work.

question: Evaluate the effectiveness of international policies, such as the Basel Convention, in preventing e-waste from polluting oceanic ecosystems. In your answer, propose a multi-faceted strategy that addresses the full lifecycle of electronics to better protect marine environments.
expectedPoints: The Basel Convention regulates the transboundary movement of hazardous waste, including e-waste, but its effectiveness is limited by non-ratification from key nations and illegal trafficking, allowing waste to still reach coastal areas.

A comprehensive strategy must include: 1) Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) laws forcing manufacturers to design for longevity and recyclability. 2) Robust domestic recycling infrastructure in all nations to eliminate the economic incentive for illegal export. 3) Consumer education programs on proper e-waste disposal to prevent landfill leaching and incineration emissions that eventually deposit into oceans. 4) International funding and technology transfer to help developing nations manage their own growing e-waste streams sustainably, protecting their local coastal environments.
